
---
title: "ARIMA Models (2)"
author: <font size="5"> Son Nguyen </font>
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, metropolis, metropolis-fonts]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      slideNumberFormat: |
        <div class="progress-bar-container">
          <div class="progress-bar" style="width: calc(%current% / %total% * 100%);">
          </div>
        </div>`
---

<style>

.remark-slide-content {
  background-color: #FFFFFF;
  border-top: 80px solid #F9C389;
  font-size: 17px;
  font-weight: 300;
  line-height: 1.5;
  padding: 1em 2em 1em 2em
}

.inverse {
  background-color: #696767;
  border-top: 80px solid #696767;
  text-shadow: none;
  background-image: url(https://github.com/goodekat/presentations/blob/master/2019-isugg-gganimate-spooky/figures/spider.png?raw=true);
	background-position: 50% 75%;
  background-size: 150px;
}

.your-turn{
  background-color: #8C7E95;
  border-top: 80px solid #F9C389;
  text-shadow: none;
  background-image: url(https://github.com/goodekat/presentations/blob/master/2019-isugg-gganimate-spooky/figures/spider.png?raw=true);
	background-position: 95% 90%;
  background-size: 75px;
}

.title-slide {
  background-color: #F9C389;
  border-top: 80px solid #F9C389;
  background-image: none;
}

.title-slide > h1  {
  color: #111111;
  font-size: 40px;
  text-shadow: none;
  font-weight: 400;
  text-align: left;
  margin-left: 15px;
  padding-top: 80px;
}
.title-slide > h2  {
  margin-top: -25px;
  padding-bottom: -20px;
  color: #111111;
  text-shadow: none;
  font-weight: 300;
  font-size: 35px;
  text-align: left;
  margin-left: 15px;
}
.title-slide > h3  {
  color: #111111;
  text-shadow: none;
  font-weight: 300;
  font-size: 25px;
  text-align: left;
  margin-left: 15px;
  margin-bottom: -30px;
}

</style>

```{css, echo=FALSE}
.left-code {
  color: #777;
  width: 48%;
  height: 92%;
  float: left;
}
.right-plot {
  width: 51%;
  float: right;
  padding-left: 1%;
}
```

```{r setup, include = FALSE}

# R markdown options
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, 
                      fig.width = 10,
                      fig.height = 5,
                      fig.align = "center", 
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE)

# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(forecast)
```


# AR(p)

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1y_{t-1}+ \beta_2y_{t-2} +....+ \beta_py_{t-p} + \epsilon$$

---
# Partial Autocorrelation

If $X$, $Y$ and $Z$ are random variables then the partial autocorrelation between $X$ and $Y$ given $Z$ is the correlation between $X$ and $Y$ with the linear effect of $Z$ removed from both $X$ and $Y$. 

- Regress $X$ on $Z$ to obtain $\hat{X}$, the linear effect of $Z$ in $X$, 
- $X -\hat{X}$ is $X$ with the linear effect of $Z$ removed 
- Regress $Y$ on $Z$ to obtain $\hat{Y}$, the linear effect of $Z$ in $Y$, 
- $Y -\hat{Y}$ is $Y$ with the linear effect of $Z$ removed 

$$
p_{XY|Z} = corr(X-\hat{X}, Y- \hat{Y})
$$

---
# PACF

- Let $p_1$ be the correlation between $y_t$ and itself, thus $p_1 =  1$

- $p_2$ be the partial autocorrelation between $y_t$ and $y_{t-2}$ given $y_{t-1}$ (removing the effect of $y_t-1$)

- $p_3$ be the partial autocorrelation between $y_t$ and $y_{t-3}$ given $y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}$ (removing the effect of $y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}$)

- And so on

---
# PACF of AR(p)

Consider an AR(2) model

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1y_{t-1}+ \beta_2y_{t-2} + \epsilon$$
- Then

$$PACF(3) =  PACF(4) = ...= 0$$

---
# Example

```{r}
yt <- arima.sim(list(order=c(3,0,0), ar=c(.1, .65, .2)), n=10000)
b0 = 10
yt <- yt + b0
pacf(yt)
```

---
# Example

```{r}
library(quantmod)
library(forecast)
getSymbols("MSFT")
yt = MSFT$MSFT.Open
yt <- yt[index(yt) > "2023-01-01"]
pacf(yt)
```

---
# Example

```{r}
# estimate the series using AR(1) model
yt_ar  = arima(yt, order = c(1,0,0))

# plot the estimated series and the original series
yt_predicted <- yt - yt_ar$residuals

plot(yt)
points(yt_predicted, type = "l", 
       col = "red", lty = 2)
```

---
# Example

```{r}
yt_forecasts <- forecast(yt_ar, h=5)
plot(yt_forecasts)
```


---
# Example

```{r}
d = read.csv('data/earthquakes.csv')   
yt = ts(d$Quakes, frequency = 1, start = 1916)
pacf(yt)
```

---
# Example

```{r}
# estimate the series using AR(1) model
yt_ar  = arima(yt, order = c(3,0,0))

# plot the estimated series and the original series
yt_predicted <- yt - yt_ar$residuals

plot(yt)
points(yt_predicted, type = "l", 
       col = "red", lty = 2)
```

---
# Example

```{r}
yt_forecasts <- forecast(yt_ar, h=5)
plot(yt_forecasts)
```


# Simple Moving Average Models - MA(1)

- Today = Mean + Noise + Slope * (Yesterday's Noise)

- Formally

$$Y_t = \mu  + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$$

where $\epsilon ~ (0, \sigma^2)$

- Three paramters: $\mu$, $\theta$ and $\sigma^2$

---
- MA models should not be confused with the MA smoothing
- A MA model is used for forecasting future values
- MA smoothing is used for estimating the trend-cycle of past values.

---
# MA(q)

$$Y_t = \mu  + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} +...+\theta_q \epsilon_{t-q} + \epsilon_t$$

where $\epsilon ~ (0, \sigma^2)$


---
# Examples

```{r}
yt <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(.6)), n=100)
b0 = 10
yt <- yt + b0
plot(yt)
```

---

```{r}
acf(yt)
```


---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

Both AR(1) and MA(1) are stationary so it is not easy to tell the different looking at the series plots 

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(.1)), n=100)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(.1)), n=100)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(y_ma)
plot(y_ar)
```

---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(.5)), n=100)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(.5)), n=100)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(y_ma)
plot(y_ar)
```


---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(-.5)), n=100)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(-.5)), n=100)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(y_ma)
plot(y_ar)
```

---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

- MA(1) has autocorrelation at lag 1 only

- AR(1) has autocorrelation at many lags


---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(.8)), n=1000)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(.8)), n=1000)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(y_ma)
acf(y_ar)
```

---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(-.8)), n=1000)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(-.8)), n=1000)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(y_ma)
acf(y_ar)
```

---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
y_ma <- arima.sim(list(order=c(0,0,1), ma=c(-.1)), n=1000)
y_ar <- arima.sim(list(order=c(1,0,0), ar=c(-.1)), n=1000)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(y_ma)
acf(y_ar)
```

---
# Forecasting with MA Models

- [Inflation Dataset](inflation.csv): monthly observations in the US from 1950-2 to 1990-12 

A time series containing :

pai1: one-month inflation rate (in percent, annual rate)

pai3: three-month inflation rate (in percent, annual rate)

tb1: one-month T-bill rate (in percent, annual rate)

tb3: three-month T-bill rate (in percent, annual rate)

cpi: CPI for urban consumers, all items (the 1982-1984 average is set to 100)



---

```{r, eval=FALSE}
# import the data
df = read.csv('data/inflation.csv')
# define the series
yt = ts(df$pai1, frequency = 12, start = c(1950, 2))
# check for stationary
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(yt)
acf(yt)
```

---

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# import the data
df = read.csv('data/inflation.csv')
# define the series
yt = ts(df$pai1, frequency = 12, start = c(1950, 2))
# check for stationary
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(yt)
acf(yt)
```
- The ACF does not die out to zero (coming into the blue strip) quickly indicates non-stationary




---

```{r}
# create the differenced series for stationary
y_diff = diff(yt)
# check for stationary
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(y_diff)
acf(y_diff)
```
- The ACF dies out to zero (coming into the blue strip) quickly indicates stationary

---

```{r}
# fit the MA(1) model to the differenced series
y_ma = arima(y_diff, order = c(0,0,1))
```


---
```{r}
# plot the fitted series
plot(yt)
lines(yt-y_ma$residuals, col = "red")
```

---
```{r}
# make predictions
d_n = forecast(y_ma, h = 1)
y_next = d_n$mean + yt[length(yt)]
y_next = as.numeric(y_next)
y_next
```

---
# AR(1) vs. MA(1)

```{r}
# fit the MA(1) model to the data
y_ma = arima(y_diff, order = c(0,0,1))
y_ar = arima(y_diff, order = c(1,0,0))

# plot the fitted series
plot(yt)
lines(yt-y_ma$residuals, col = "red")
lines(yt-y_ar$residuals, col = "blue")
```
